item.source_excerpt: When the helper produces no output (absent, denied, unavailable interpreter, or an unignored/unwritable session directory) both render `missing` and the run continues; the editing gate is untouched.

This enumerates the exact four no-output causes (absent, denied, unavailable interpreter, unignored/unwritable session directory) and confirms both facts render missing with the run continuing. Line 118: 'The editing gate is affirmative-only and consults the post-update head-match verdict: it bars IMPLEMENT only when the subject is PR-bound and that verdict is mismatch, or when the subject is ambiguous; match, advanced, and a head-match fact whose status is missing never bar.' This confirms the gate bars only on mismatch or ambiguous. The overview's claim matches the skill's contract exactly.","file_checked":"skills/receiving-code-review/SKILL.md:106,118","property_proven":true,"inaccuracy_scope":"none"}

item.source_excerpt: the derived value is byte-equal to `git rev-parse HEAD^{tree}` after that commit - commit-invariant across the recorded content - while any tracked-content change after the verified state compares unequal.

pairs.response_text:
{
  "id": "VC-8",
  "verdict": "PASS",
  "evidence": "Constructed falsifying attempts against scripts/reception_identity.py's derive_candidate_identity() in a scratch repo at /tmp/idtest*: (1) rename-only (git mv a.txt b.txt): derived pre-commit identity matched direct git write-tree and HEAD^{tree} after commit (dd5a3627...) once the scratch dir was clean of extraneous untracked files. (2) mode-only change (chmod +x under core.fileMode=true): derived value b1f73f0b... equaled HEAD^{tree} after `git commit -am`. (3) repo with no prior commits, untracked content: derived value on first commit (`git add -A && git commit`) equaled HEAD^{tree} (2e811714...). (4) a further tracked-content change after the verified state produced a different derived value (d4e01edf...) than the unchanged HEAD^{tree} (2e811714...), confirming the 'compares unequal' half. No case falsified the universal; the implementation's `git add -A` + temp-index `write-tree` (scripts/reception_identity.py lines ~103-125) captures path, blob content, and mode identically to what a `git add -A && git commit` records, so post-commit HEAD^{tree} is byte-equal to the pre-commit derivation, and any subsequent tracked edit changes the tree hash.",
  "file_checked": "scripts/reception_identity.py:88-134",
  "property_proven": true,
  "inaccuracy_scope": "none"
}

item.source_excerpt: The repository's own index is never modified and no history is read.

pairs.response_text: {"id": "VC-9", "verdict": "PASS", "evidence": "Read scripts/reception_identity.py in full. Every git call that could touch the index either (a) rev-parse --absolute-git-dir at line 101, which does not read or write the index at all, or (b) the add -A / write-tree calls at lines 121-122, which pass extra_env={'GIT_INDEX_FILE': tmp_index} into _run_git; since _run_git does env=os.environ.copy(); env.update(extra_env) (lines 70-72), this dict-update always overrides any ambient GIT_INDEX_FILE for those two calls, redirecting them onto the private temp index, never the real one. The seeding step (line 114) only shutil.copyfile()s FROM the real index path TO the temp path (read-only on the real file). No git call reads commit history (no `log`, `rev-list`, `cat-file <commit>`, etc.); write-tree only touches the object database (blobs/trees), not history. I constructed two adversarial tests in a scratch repo (/private/tmp/ridtest): (1) baseline call with staged/unstaged/untracked changes present, comparing .git/index md5 and mtime before/after derive_candidate_identity() -- both identical (md5 2cb51dc4a547c4a47b35d9b2a038c1af, mtime 1784648961 unchanged). (2) adversarial run with GIT_INDEX_FILE explicitly exported pointing AT the real index before calling the function (simulating an inherited ambient GIT_INDEX_FILE) -- the real index was still untouched (same md5/mtime), because the add-A/write-tree extra_env override wins. Both tests confirm the authored claim holds, including under the specific adversarial scenario the verify_hint raised.", "file_checked": "scripts/reception_identity.py:64-134", "property_proven": true, "inaccuracy_scope": "none"}

item.source_excerpt: Every failure mode raises a named breadcrumb and yields no identity.

pairs.response_text: {"id": "VC-10", "verdict": "PASS", "evidence": "Enumerated every raise site in scripts/reception_identity.py: git_not_found (FileNotFoundError, line 82), git_exec_error:<class> (other OSError from subprocess.run, line 84), git_failed:<subcommand>:<code> (non-zero returncode, line 88), temp_index_error:<class> (mkstemp OSError, line 109, and OSError during add/write-tree, line 124), empty_tree_output (write-tree stdout empty after strip, line 133). Every one of these is a distinct IdentityError subclass instance carrying a named .reason string (never a bare/generic Exception, never a raw traceback). No path returns a bare string or prints anything on failure: derive_candidate_identity either returns `tree` (only reached after the empty-tree check passes) or raises IdentityError; there is no intermediate print/return of an unvalidated value. Attempted falsifiers: (1) git absent -> FileNotFoundError -> git_not_found, named. (2) git rc!=0 (e.g. rev-parse outside a repo, or `add`/`write-tree` failing) -> git_failed:<subcommand>:<code>, named. (3) unwritable temp index (mkstemp fails) -> temp_index_error:<class>, named. (4) no-commits repo -> rev-parse --absolute-git-dir still succeeds (git dir exists pre-first-commit) and write-tree on an index with no HEAD still succeeds and prints a valid non-empty tree hash, so this is not actually a failure mode of this routine (falsifier attempt fails to find a bare-value leak). (5) empty tree output -> explicit empty_tree_output raise, named; unreachable in normal git behavior (write-tree always prints a 40/64-hex tree id on success) but the guard exists and is named regardless. (6) generic git exec OSError (permissions, ENOMEM, etc.) -> git_exec_error:<class>, named. Checked the sole caller scripts/reception-record.py:200-201, which catches ri.IdentityError explicitly rather than letting a bare value leak through on failure. No falsifying path found: every raise carries the named reason vocabulary the docstring/overview claims, and no failure path returns/prints an unnamed or bare value.", "file_checked": "scripts/reception_identity.py:50-134", "property_proven": true, "inaccuracy_scope": "none"}

(1) All three artifact writes (identity, findings, pointer) are inside one try/except OSError block (lines ~232-236); any mid-sequence failure raises before sys.stdout.write is reached, so stdout is never written on a partial-write path -- verified empirically: pre-creating the findings path as a directory (forcing an OSError) yielded rc=1, empty stdout, and stderr {\"ok\": false, \"reason\": ...IsADirectoryError}. (2) The ignore-check-failed arm (_check_ignored returning None, e.g. git rc 128) returns _fail(...) before any writes and before any stdout emission -- verified empirically in a non-git directory: rc=1, empty stdout, stderr {\"ok\": false, \"reason\": \"identity:git_failed:rev-parse:128\"}. (3) Every error path in the file funnels through _fail(), which writes only to stderr via json.dumps({\"ok\": false, ...}) and returns a nonzero code; sys.stdout.write is called only once per subcommand, guarded to run solely after a successful try block. No code path writes to stdout on any failure/missing-output arm, so no arm could be read as producing a derived identity on stdout.", "file_checked": "scripts/reception-record.py:213-320 (cmd_record, cmd_append_disposition, _fail)", "property_proven": true, "inaccuracy_scope": "none"}

item.source_excerpt: The repository's own index is never modified (the derivation writes to a private `GIT_INDEX_FILE`), and no repository history is read

pairs.response_text: {"id": "VC-18", "verdict": "PASS", "evidence": "Empirically falsified the claim by construction. Built a scratch git repo with staged/unstaged/untracked changes, recorded the real .git/index checksum+mtime, ran derive_candidate_identity(), and confirmed the index was byte-identical and mtime-unchanged afterward. Repeated with an ambient GIT_INDEX_FILE env var pointing at the real index exported before calling the function: still unchanged, because _run_git (reception_identity.py:70-72) does `env = os.environ.copy(); env.update(extra_env)` and the add-A/write-tree calls always pass extra_env={'GIT_INDEX_FILE': tmp_index} (line 118), so an inherited ambient GIT_INDEX_FILE is always overridden for those two calls. The only git invocation without extra_env is `rev-parse --absolute-git-dir` (line 101), which does not touch any index. index_path (line 102) is only read via shutil.copyfile (line 114, read-only) to seed the temp index, never written. Only three git subcommands run total: rev-parse --absolute-git-dir, add -A, write-tree -- none read repository history (no log/rev-list/etc). Both halves of the docstring's claim hold.", "file_checked": "scripts/reception_identity.py:64-134", "property_proven": true, "inaccuracy_scope": "none"}

{"id": "VC-19", "verdict": "PASS", "evidence": "scripts/reception_identity.py:101-102 resolves git_dir via `git rev-parse --absolute-git-dir` and hardcodes index_path=git_dir/index, then copies that file (line 114) as the temp-index seed if it exists, else falls back to an empty index (line 117). Verified live in this worktree (a linked worktree at .git/worktrees/pr-681): `git rev-parse --absolute-git-dir` returns /Users/the01geek/repos/devflow-autopilot/.git/worktrees/pr-681 (distinct from `--git-common-dir` = .../.git), and that per-worktree dir does contain its own `index` file (90726 bytes) -- confirming the code correctly targets the per-worktree index for linked worktrees, not the common dir. Separately verified via a direct subprocess probe that setting an ambient GIT_INDEX_FILE env var does not change what `--absolute-git-dir` resolves to. However, _run_git (lines 71-89) passes os.environ.copy() as the base env for the --absolute-git-dir call, and the code never reads os.environ.get('GIT_INDEX_FILE') when constructing index_path (line 102) -- it unconditionally uses git_dir/index. So if a caller's process already has GIT_INDEX_FILE set (pointing at a different index file), the code silently ignores it and seeds from the real git_dir/index instead, exactly the discrepancy the claim describes. The claim accurately states both assumptions embedded in the code.", "file_checked": "scripts/reception_identity.py:92-134", "property_proven": true, "inaccuracy_scope": "none"}

{"id": "VC-20", "verdict": "PASS", "evidence": "Empirically verified with a scratch repo (/private/tmp/gittest): a fresh temp index seeded from the real repo index, then `git add -A` invoked with cwd=sub/ (a subdirectory), staged root2.txt (an untracked file at repo root) into the temp index alongside sub/b.txt -- confirmed via `git -C <root> ls-files -s` on the temp index. This is Git >=2.0 behavior: `git add -A` with no pathspec is repo-wide (equivalent to `git add -A :/`), not scoped to cwd, unlike pre-2.0 git. scripts/reception_identity.py:121 calls `_run_git([\"add\", \"-A\"], cwd, env)` with no pathspec, matching this repo-wide semantics, and its docstring (lines 14-16, 119-120) explicitly documents 'git add -A stages every working-tree content change' -- consistent with the actual observed behavior. The derive_candidate_identity function's `cwd` (line 99) defaults to os.getcwd() and is passed straight through, so if invoked from a subdirectory the resulting identity is still derived over the whole repo tree, not a cwd-scoped subset -- matching the claim.", "file_checked": "scripts/reception_identity.py:99-134", "property_proven": true, "inaccuracy_scope": "none"}

This is charset-only validation with no upper length bound, so a 4000-char hex string passes and would form a valid (if oddly long) filename via _paths() (line 186-191) with no path-traversal risk since the charset excludes '/' and the token cannot start with '-' given hex-only chars. {"id": "VC-32", "verdict": "FAIL", "evidence": "The claim asserts SESSION_DIRNAME's os.path.join produces backslash separators that break git check-ignore / POSIX expectations, but this mismatches the actual design. First, git check-ignore's own resolution is separator-tolerant (git for Windows accepts native backslash paths), and the docstring at reception-record.py:167-168 explicitly notes it is 'never a path-shape assumption.' Second, and decisively per the verify_hint's fail-direction test: _check_ignored (lines 163-183) returns None on any OSError or unresolvable git return code (line 177-178, 183), and the sole caller cmd_record (lines 212-218) treats None as a hard failure via _fail('ignore_check_failed:git-could-not-resolve-ignore-state') BEFORE any artifact write occurs (comment at line 211: 'Confirm the session directory is ignored BEFORE writing'). So any hypothetical Windows path-resolution problem fails CLOSED (refuses with a named breadcrumb), not OPEN (writing to a non-ignored path) - the opposite of what the claim's framing implies risk of. The dependency-interaction premise (separator mismatch causing unsafe behavior) is unsubstantiated by the code.", "file_checked": "scripts/reception-record.py:57,163-218", "property_proven": false, "inaccuracy_scope": "generated_claim_text"}

The six-shape matrix (object_valid/array/scalar/valid_falsy/missing/wrong_type_findings) is applied ONLY to the append-disposition read-back (_append helper reading {token}.findings.json). This is explicitly confirmed by the file's own deferred-coverage-gaps comment block (lines 495-512): 'the record idempotent read-back (existing_findings_*) against the six-shape matrix, which is currently applied only to the append-disposition read-back' is listed as an untested/deferred arm. So the record-idempotent read-back path lacks matrix coverage entirely (not just missing an empty-string identity-token row). The claim's specific assertion about an additional required 'empty-string identity token row' for valid-falsy coverage is not something this test file's docstring or content substantiates or refutes directly (no such AC text is present in the file to cross-check), but the core observation that the matrix's valid-falsy row is JSON-false-only and coverage is scoped to append-disposition (not record's existing_findings_* read-back) is accurate.", "file_checked": "lib/test/test_reception_identity.py:409-432,495-512", "property_proven": true, "inaccuracy_scope": "none"}

{"id": "VC-34", "verdict": "FAIL", "evidence": "scripts/reception-record.py cmd_record (lines 194-274) re-derives candidate_identity via ri.derive_candidate_identity(cwd) at the top of every invocation (line 200) and unconditionally overwrites the identity artifact via _atomic_write_json(identity_path, identity_record) (line 257) even when --token matches an existing session (only the findings ledger is preserved, lines 229-245). This means a reused token whose working tree changed between invocations gets a silently rebound identity_record written to disk - not idempotent for the identity artifact. The module docstring itself (lines 16-24) was edited in this PR to explicitly disclaim exactly this: 'Idempotent in shape for an existing --token... the identity artifact is rewritten with a FRESHLY re-derived candidate identity - equal to the recorded one only for an unchanged working tree. A re-record after an edit therefore rebinds the token to the new content identity by design.' This directly contradicts the AC's stated requirement quoted in verify_against ('idempotent for the identity artifact') and the claim's characterization. Nothing in cmd_record's code path warns, diffs against the prior identity, or surfaces the rebind to the caller/consumer - the caller only sees stdout JSON with the new candidate_identity, identical in shape to a first invocation.", "file_checked": "scripts/reception-record.py:194-274", "property_proven": true, "inaccuracy_scope": "source_authored_text"}
